questions.question: Which of the following are perturbation methods? (Select all that apply)
questions.explanation: Perturbation methods disrupt brain function to test causality. These include brain lesions, TMS (virtual lesions), tDCS, optogenetics, and pharmacology. EEG and fMRI are neuromonitoring (observational) methods.

questions.question: Which of the following are true about TMS? (Select all that apply)

options.B: Its effects are transient and reversible
options.C: It can target deep brain structures
options.D: It can only reach about 1.5 cm depth

questions.explanation: TMS creates virtual lesions that are transient, can only reach superficial cortex (~1.5 cm), and carries safety risks (e.g., seizures in rare cases). It is a perturbation method that tests causality, not just correlational evidence, and cannot reach deep structures.

questions.question: Which of the following are limitations of brain lesion studies? (Select all that apply)

options.A: Lesions produce variable damage across patients
options.B: Diaschisis can affect remote brain regions
options.C: Lesions are too small to study
options.D: The brain may reorganise after damage
options.E: Overlapping lesion analysis can help localise function

questions.explanation: Lesion studies are limited by variable damage patterns, diaschisis (remote effects), and neuroplastic reorganisation. Lesions are typically not too small — they are often imprecise and too large. Overlapping lesion analysis is a method to address some limitations, not itself a limitation.

questions.question: Which statements about fMRI are correct? (Select all that apply)

options.D: It can use block or event-related designs
options.E: The BOLD response peaks at 5-6 seconds
options.F: It directly measures neural electrical activity

questions.explanation: fMRI measures the BOLD signal with millimetre spatial resolution, can use block or event-related designs, and the BOLD response peaks at 5-6 seconds. It does NOT have millisecond temporal resolution (that is EEG) and it does NOT directly measure neural activity — it measures blood oxygenation changes.

questions.question: Which of the following are true about optogenetics? (Select all that apply)

options.B: It offers very precise spatial and temporal control
options.C: It is non-invasive and safe for humans
options.D: It is currently limited to animal studies
options.E: It can target specific neuron types

questions.explanation: Optogenetics combines genetic manipulation with light delivery to control specific neuron types with high precision. It is invasive (requires genetic modification and implanted light sources) and currently limited to animal research.

questions.question: Which methods are affected by the inverse problem? (Select all that apply)
questions.explanation: The inverse problem specifically applies to EEG and MEG because both record signals at the surface (scalp) and must infer the brain sources that generated those signals. Multiple source configurations can produce the same surface pattern. fMRI and PET directly localise activity; TMS is a stimulation method.

questions.question: Which of the following are connectivity analysis methods used with fMRI data? (Select all that apply)
questions.explanation: Functional connectivity, PPI, DCM, and SEM are all methods for analysing connectivity between brain regions using fMRI data. MVPA is a pattern classification approach, not a connectivity analysis method.

questions.question: Which of the following are advantages of MEG over EEG? (Select all that apply)

options.A: Magnetic fields are not distorted by skull and tissue
options.B: MEG is much cheaper than EEG
options.D: MEG completely solves the inverse problem
options.E: Magnetic signals pass through tissue without smearing

questions.explanation: MEG's key advantage is that magnetic fields pass through skull and tissue without distortion or smearing, unlike EEG's electrical signals. This gives MEG better source localisation potential. However, MEG is much more expensive than EEG and does NOT solve the inverse problem — it still applies.

questions.question: Which of the following correctly describe EEG? (Select all that apply)

options.C: It has poor spatial resolution due to signal smearing
options.D: ERPs are derived from EEG through time-locked averaging
options.E: It measures post-synaptic potentials of pyramidal cells

questions.explanation: EEG has excellent temporal resolution (milliseconds), poor spatial resolution (signal smearing through skull), and measures post-synaptic potentials of pyramidal cells (NOT action potentials — this is a classic exam trap). ERPs are indeed extracted from EEG via time-locked averaging.

questions.question: Which principles are essential for drawing strong conclusions in cognitive neuroscience? (Select all that apply)

options.B: Complementarity of methods with different strengths
options.C: Double dissociations rather than single associations
options.D: Relying on a single gold-standard method

questions.explanation: Strong conclusions require convergence (multiple methods pointing to the same conclusion), complementarity (combining methods with different strengths/weaknesses), double dissociations (stronger than simple associations), and combining perturbation with monitoring. No single method is a 'gold standard'.
